mnemonics.title: CLASSIFY
mnemonics.content: C-L-A-S-S-I-F-Y: Convolutional Layers Analyze Specific Spatial Features Yields classification. Remember: CNNs extract hierarchical features from edges to complex patterns for categorization.

mnemonics.title: YOLO Fast Detection
mnemonics.content: YOLO: You Only Look Once - Remember by thinking of a security guard quickly glancing once to identify all objects in a frame in real-time.

mnemonics.title: R-CNN Family
mnemonics.content: R-CNN Evolution: R-CNN → Fast R-CNN → Faster R-CNN → Mask R-CNN. Remember: 'Regional proposals get Faster and Masked progressively' - each version improved speed and added capabilities.

mnemonics.title: Segmentation Types
mnemonics.content: SIP: Semantic (pixel class), Instance (distinguish instances), Panoptic (both together). Remember: 'Simply Identify Pixels' - each level adds more detail.

mnemonics.title: GAN Components
mnemonics.content: GAN: Generator (creates fake) vs Discriminator (judges real). Remember: 'Generator Acts Naively, Discriminator Judges' - they compete like artist vs critic.

mnemonics.title: OCR Process
mnemonics.content: OCR Pipeline: Preprocess → Text Detection → Text Recognition → Post-processing. Remember: 'Pick The Red Pepper' - Preprocess, Text Detection, Text Recognition, Post-processing.

mnemonics.title: Pose Estimation Keypoints
mnemonics.content: Human Pose: Detect body joints (ankles, knees, hips, shoulders, elbows, wrists, neck, head). Remember: 'All Kids Have Great Elastic Wrist Needles' - Ankles, Knees, Hips, Head, Elbows, Wrists, Neck.

mnemonics.title: Self-Driving Sensors
mnemonics.content: AV Sensors: Cameras, LiDAR, Radar, Ultrasonic. Remember: 'CLRU' - Cameras, LiDAR, Radar, Ultrasonic for comprehensive perception.

acronyms.usage: Used for image classification, detection, and segmentation tasks. Core architecture for most CV applications.
Object detection framework that uses region proposals. Foundation for Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN, and Mask R-CNN.
Real-time object detection algorithm. Processes entire image in one forward pass, ideal for speed-critical applications.
Object detection that performs detection in a single forward pass using feature pyramids.
Image generation using generator-discriminator architecture. Applications: deepfakes, art generation, data augmentation.
Converting images of text into machine-readable text. Used in document scanning, license plate recognition.
Robotics: Building map while tracking location. Essential for autonomous vehicles and robots.
Image segmentation architecture that uses only convolutional layers, enabling pixel-wise predictions.
Deep CNN with skip connections. Used as backbone for many CV tasks due to training stability.
Deep CNN architecture (VGG-16, VGG-19). Popular benchmark for image classification.
CNN with parallel convolutions of different sizes (inception modules). Efficient feature extraction.
Metric for object detection accuracy. Measures overlap between predicted and ground truth bounding boxes.
Standard metric for object detection. Higher mAP = better model performance.
Post-processing in object detection to remove duplicate boxes. Keeps best detection per object.
Traditional feature extraction (now mostly replaced by deep learning). Keypoint detection and description.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a flashlight (kernel) sliding across a photo (image), highlighting specific patterns at each position. The flashlight scans systematically, transforming what it sees into numerical values at each step.
Think of police chalk outlines at crime scenes - boxes drawn around objects to identify and locate them precisely within the image frame.
Imagine color-coded floor plans where each room type has a specific color - kitchens blue, bedrooms red, bathrooms green - every pixel labeled by what it represents.
Picture a counterfeiter (Generator) trying to fool a detective (Discriminator). Over time, the counterfeiter gets so good that even experts cannot tell fake from real.
Think of looking at a city from different heights - ground level (fine details), from a building (medium), from airplane (overall layout) - each level captures different information.
Imagine a stick figure puppet with joints connected by strings. Each joint is a keypoint, and the connections form the skeleton that represents human pose.
Think of a Rubik's cube representing an image: height × width × color channels (RGB). Each small square holds a numerical value representing color intensity.
Imagine someone who learned to ride a bicycle (pretrained model) learning to ride a motorcycle (new task) - they already know balance and coordination, just need to adapt.
Think of reading a crowded photo - your eyes naturally focus on important regions while ignoring background clutter. Attention does the same for neural networks.
Imagine different sized fishing nets dropped over an area - some are large (for big fish/objects), some small (for small fish/objects). The network adjusts these predefined boxes to match objects.

analogies.analogy: Like a museum curator categorizing paintings into genres (impressionism, abstract, portrait) based on visual characteristics learned over time.
Like a security guard monitoring multiple screens, simultaneously identifying and locating people, vehicles, and objects with bounding boxes around each.
Like an architect's floor plan where every pixel is labeled as wall, floor, door, or window - not just identifying objects but labeling every single area.
Like a seasoned bartender who recognizes regular customers by their unique facial features, even after not seeing them for months.
Like having a sighted friend describe a photograph to you in complete detail - identifying objects, actions, and relationships between elements.
Like a talented artist who can paint the same scene in different artistic styles (Van Gogh, Picasso, Monet) - preserving content while transforming artistic style.
Like an experienced driver using mirrors, eyes, and intuition to understand the road, pedestrians, other vehicles, and obstacles simultaneously.
Like a radiologist with superhuman abilities, instantly detecting tumors, fractures, and abnormalities across thousands of X-rays, MRIs, and CT scans without fatigue.
Like a sports commentator following a specific player throughout a match, maintaining identity despite changes in appearance, lighting, and viewpoint.
Like a forensic artist enhancing a blurry surveillance image to reveal clear facial features - upscaling while adding realistic details.
Like humans using two eyes to perceive depth - the brain calculates how far objects are by comparing the slightly different views from each eye.
Like a teacher creating different versions of the same question (different phrasings, rotated images, varied contexts) to help students generalize better.
